coredata.dc:title: Advances in the application of carbon dots-based fluorescent probes in disease biomarker detection
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.prism:publisher: Elsevier B.V. All rights are reserved, including those for text and data mining, AI training, and similar technologies.
coredata.dc:creator: Zhang, Haoqi
Zhang, Qingmei
Li, Naihui
Yang, Guoqing
Cheng, Zewei
Du, Xiujuan
Sun, Lingxiang
Wang, Wei
Li, Bing
coredata.dc:description: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging nanomaterial, have shown tremendous potential in disease biomarker detection. CDs can selectively interact with different target molecules, enabling highly sensitive and specific detection of these biomolecules. Compared to traditional detection methods, CDs sensors offer advantages such as rapid response, high detection sensitivity, and low cost. In this review, we summarize the latest advances in the application of CDs fluorescence probes for the detection of disease biomarkers, including sensing mechanisms, and their applications in the selective detection of metal ions, amino acids, enzymes, proteins, other biomolecules, as well as bacteria and viruses. We discuss the current challenges and issues associated with the practical application of CDs-based fluorescent probes. Furthermore, we propose future directions for the development of CDs. We hope that this review will provide new insights for researchers in the field of disease biomarker detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CDs
Fluorescent probe
Disease biomarker
Sensing

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Detecting metal ions. (a) Schematic diagram illustrating the fluorescence quenching mechanism of N-CDs in the presence of Fe3+ ions [43]. Copyright 2017, Elsevier. (b) The synthesis of CDs and imaging of zebrafish and mouse cells [44]. Copyright 2021, Elsevier. (c) Schematic diagram of EGTA-modified CDs for detecting Ca2+ [45]. Copyright 2019, American Chemical Society. (d) Potassium ion K+ detection principle [46]. Copyright 2019, Elsevier.

Detecting amino acids.(a) Switchable dual-functional sensing platform for Fe3+ ions and L-cysteine [59]. Copyright 2019, American Chemical Society. (b) The synthesis of CDs and the fluorescence detection mechanism of L-Glu [60]. Copyright 2022, Elsevier. (c) Schematic diagram of a sensor for detecting Ag+ and GSH, as well as its application in bioimaging [61]. Copyright 2019, American Chemical Society.

Detection enzyme. (a) Preparation and Detection Mechanism of Ratio Fluorescence Probe for PTK7 [64]. Copyright 2021,Journal of Nanobiotechnology. (b) The CDs-based ratiometric sensor for AChE and BChE detection [65]. Copyright 2019, Elsevier. (c) Working principle for ALP sensing based on inner filter effect [66]. Copyright 2016, American Chemical Society.

Detecting proteins. (a) CDs were conjugated to DNA and then the CDs-Apta fluorescence response quenched by AuNPs-PAMAM-Ab through FRET in the presence of CA125 analyte [74]. Copyright 2017, Elsevier. (b) Schematic diagram of detecting CEA via CDs [76]. Copyright 2016, Elsevier. (c) NIR-CDs-based fluorescence aptasensor for target CEA [78].Copyright 2019, Elsevier. (d) CDs/GO and CHA signal amplification strategy based on FRET for the detection system of PSA [79].

Detecting small biomolecules. (a) Schematic diagram of detecting glucose based on Fe3O4@APBA [80]. Copyright 2019, Elsevier. (b) Schematic diagram of dual-mode ratiometric fluorescence and colorimetric strategy for glucose analysis based on bifunctiona Fe,Co-CQD [81]. Copyright 2022, Elsevier. (c) Schematic diagram of the detection strategy for DA using a biosensor based on CDs-Apt/NG [82]. Copyright 2016, Elsevier. (d) A portable smartphone platform based on a single dual-emission ratio fluorescence probe for visual detection of isopropanol in exhaled breath [83]. Copyright 2021, American Chemical Society.

Detection bacterial.(a) Schematic diagram of the coupling process between CDs-apt complex and bacteria [86]. Copyright 2015, Royal Society of Chemistry. (b) Diagram for detecting Staphylococcus aureus [87]. Copyright 2015, Elsevier. (c) A schematic diagram for the detection of Staphylococcus aureus using nitrogen and chlorine-doped carbon quantum dots modified with vancomycin [88]. Copyright 2023, Elsevier. (d) Innovative method for pathogen detection vs conventional method for pathogen detection [89]. Copyright 2018, American Chemical Society. (e) A schematic diagram illustrating the principle of a fluorescent aptamer-based sensor for detecting Pseudomonas aeruginosa [90]. Copyright 2018, American Chemical Society. (f) The synthesis and application of N-CDs-GP [93]. Copyright 2020, Elsevier.

Detection virus. (a) Schematic diagram illustrating the design principle of a HPV-18 DNA biosensor based on probe DNA immobilization and target DNA hybridization onto N-CDs modified ITO glass electrode [101]. Copyright 2021, American Chemical Society. (b) Schematic diagram of ultra-sensitive detection of SFTSV based on immune fluorescence CDs/SiO₂ nanosphere lateral flow chromatography [102]. Copyright 2019, American Chemical Society. (c) Diagram for Norovirus Detection [103].Copyright 2019, Journal of Biotechnology Engineering. (d) A fluorescence detection platform composed of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) for virus detection [106]. Copyright 2020, Elsevier.
Summary of CDs for metal ion detection.

head.ce:title: Advances in the application of carbon dots-based fluorescent probes in disease biomarker detection

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon dots (CDs), as an emerging nanomaterial, have shown tremendous potential in disease biomarker detection. CDs can selectively interact with different target molecules, enabling highly sensitive and specific detection of these biomolecules. Compared to traditional detection methods, CDs sensors offer advantages such as rapid response, high detection sensitivity, and low cost. In this review, we summarize the latest advances in the application of CDs fluorescence probes for the detection of disease biomarkers, including sensing mechanisms, and their applications in the selective detection of metal ions, amino acids, enzymes, proteins, other biomolecules, as well as bacteria and viruses. We discuss the current challenges and issues associated with the practical application of CDs-based fluorescent probes. Furthermore, we propose future directions for the development of CDs. We hope that this review will provide new insights for researchers in the field of disease biomarker detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: No data was used for the research described in the article.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs) have attracted considerable attention as a new type of fluorescent carbon nanomaterial because of their excellent optical properties, biocompatibility, and high electrical conductivity. CDs mainly refer to graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymer dots (CPDs) [1]. They were first purified from soot generated using the arc discharge method through physical means in 2004 by Xu et al. [2]. Two years later, Sun et al. synthesized fluorescent carbon nanoparticles using a laser ablation method with graphite as the carbon source, and they coined the term "carbon dots [3]. The surface of CDs is rich in various functional groups, which determine their physical, chemical, and fluorescent properties, including quantum yield, emission wavelength, aggregation-induced emission/quenching, fluorescence lifetime, biocompatibility, and responsiveness to specific substances. CDs are a novel type of fluorescent probe, material that exhibits unique advantages over traditional organic probes. The main advantages of CDs: include better biocompatibility, higher stability, low toxicity, simple synthesis, and a wide range of excitation wavelengths. Consequently, they have been applied to sensing [4–6], bioimaging, and fluorescence nanoprobes for environmental and biological detection [7–10].

Before this, a large number of reviews extensively described and discussed the synthesis of fluorescent CDs [11–18], functionalization [19,20], luminescence mechanisms [1,21], properties, and applications of fluorescent CDs [22–33]. In 2022, Lu et al. published a review detailing CDs breakthroughs regarding synthesis, modification, optical properties, toxicology, and biocatalytic platforms [32]. They extensively discussed the latest research on CDs in applications such as biological imaging, biosensing, and drug delivery, as well as antimicrobial, anticancer (including photothermal therapy, photodynamic therapy, and combination therapies), and antiviral treatments. In 2024, Zhou et al. provided an overview highlighting the advantages of biomass-derived CDs and their applications in optical imaging, sensing, and phototherapy [31]. Recently, Li et al. reviewed the advancements in the research of CDs-based fluorescent biosensors for early disease diagnosis [33]. The authors systematically summarised various analysis strategies for integrating CDs-based fluorescent biosensors with different recognition elements for disease markers detection, mainly including direct identification, identification through medium, enzyme, antibody, aptamer, and molecularly imprinted polymer (MIP). This review offers new insights into the rational design of CDs-based fluorescent probes. Given the importance of biosensors, this review elaborates the latest advancements in the application of CDs fluorescent probes for detecting disease biomarkers. It discusses sensing mechanisms and their selective detection applications for metal ions, amino acids, enzymes, proteins, other biomolecules, bacteria, and viruses. It is hoped that this study will inspire researchers to delve deeper into the practical applications of CDs in disease biomarker detection, thereby fully harnessing their potential value.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Sensing mechanisms of fluorescent probes based on CDs
ce:section.ce:para: Based on the interaction between the recognition group and the analyte, the design strategies of CDs fluorescent probes are mainly divided into two categories: supramolecular interactions and chemical reactions. The identification group recognizes analytes through supramolecular interactions such as hydrogen bonding, electrostatic interactions, coordination, antigen antibody interactions, and specific binding of nucleic acid aptamers to target molecules. In addition, identifying functional groups can also determine whether the analyte undergoes specific chemical reactions, such as catalytic reactions, oxidation-reduction reactions, addition reactions, protection and deprotection reactions, thereby causing changes in the spectral properties of fluorescent groups [34].

Generally, there are five main sensing mechanisms for CDs-based fluorescence probes. They are Photo-induced electron transfer (PET), Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET), Internal filtering effect (IFE), Aggregation-caused quenching (ACQ), and Aggregation-induced emission (AIE).

Photo-induced electron transfer (PET) refers to the phenomenon where electrons are transferred between a donor and an acceptor under the influence of light. In the PET process, both ground state and excited state molecules can serve as electron donors or acceptors. When excitation light irradiates the probe molecule, electrons transfer from the donor to the excited fluorophore, resulting in the quenching of the excited state molecule and the conversion of light energy into chemical energy.

Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) is a non-radiative energy transfer process between an energy donor and an acceptor. The occurrence of the FRET process requires certain conditions: first, there must be a relative orientation of the dipole-dipole interaction between the donor and the acceptor; second, there should be an appropriate distance between the energy donor and the acceptor, generally within 2–8 nm; furthermore, there must be a high degree of overlap between the donor's emission spectrum and the acceptor's absorption spectrum. Only when these three conditions are met can the FRET process occur effectively.

Internal filtering effect (IFE) refers to the phenomenon where the fluorescence of a fluorophore is diminished due to the absorption of excitation or emission light by other absorbing substances present [34]. IFE also depends on the spectral overlap between the fluorophore and the absorber. Unlike FRET, IFE originates from radiative energy transfer, with the energy disturbance of the donor primarily occurring in the ground state. Therefore, only fluorescence quenching is observed in IFE, and there is no significant change in fluorescence lifetime. Additionally, another key difference from FRET is that there are no distance requirements between the energy donor and the absorber in IFE.

The phenomenon where a fluorophore exhibits high fluorescence intensity in dilute solutions but shows reduced or even absent fluorescence intensity in concentrated solutions or solid states is known as the Aggregation-caused quenching effect (ACQ) [35]. Most researchers believe that the ACQ phenomenon arises due to self-quenching caused by molecular collisions and stacking as the concentration increases, or due to interactions between quenchers and fluorophores in the system at higher concentrations. Factors such as π-π stacking, hydrogen bonding, hydrophobic effects, and electrostatic interactions can all influence this process.

Aggregation-Induced Emission (AIE) is a special luminescent phenomenon that is completely opposite to the ACQ effect. AIE materials typically do not emit light in dilute solutions, but their luminescence significantly enhances when they aggregate or are in a solid state. This phenomenon occurs because the dynamic intramolecular rotation of molecules in dilute solutions dissipates their excited-state energy. In the aggregated state, the rotation within the molecules is greatly hindered, leading to the suppression of non-radiative energy loss and the enhancement of fluorescence.

Metal ions are ubiquitous in the natural environment, and their detection is crucial in environmental and biomedical applications. The concentration changes of specific metal ions can reflect the pathological state of the body, providing potential means for early diagnosis and disease monitoring. For instance, excessive accumulation of iron ions is associated with thalassemia and cardiovascular diseases, and their detection can help assess the risk of these conditions. Abnormal copper ion metabolism is linked to Alzheimer’s disease and liver disease, and monitoring copper levels aids in the early identification of these diseases. Therefore, detecting these metal ions can serve as effective biomarkers to assist clinical diagnosis and treatment decisions. Traditional detection methods include inductively coupled plasma mass spectrometry and atomic absorption/emission spectroscopy. However, these methods require expensive equipment and complex detection procedures [36–38]. Therefore, there is a need for a simple, efficient, and low-cost detection method. CDs, a new fluorescent probe material, have become a research hotspot in metal ions detection due to their high fluorescence quantum yield, tunable emission wavelengths, low toxicity, and good biocompatibility.

Fe3+ is one of the most important transition metal ions in biological organisms and systems, and it is also one of the most abundant metal cations in the natural environment. At the cellular level, Fe3+ is involved in catalytic processes, oxygen exchange and transport, enzyme-catalysed reactions, and other biological processes [39]. Additionally, Fe3+ deficiency and excess can adversely affect immunity, leading to various serious illnesses including anaemia, heart disease, kidney damage, cancer, and cognitive impairment [40,41]. Certainly, the detection of Fe 3+ is crucial. Nagaraj et al. utilised palm leaf as a carbon source and synthesised CDs via a hydrothermal method. The synthesised CDs exhibited high sensitivity and selectivity towards Fe3+, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 2.01 μM and a linear range of 10–100 μM. The prepared CDs effectively detected Fe3+ ions in tap and drinking water, with a minimum recovery rate of 98 % [42]. The research results demonstrate that the synthesised CDs exhibit strong fluorescence, good stability, and high quantum yield. Therefore, they can serve as promising sensing materials for the fluorescence detection of Fe3+ ions. Similar reports include the work of Atchudan et al. who synthesised nitrogen-doped CDs (N-CDs) using bayberry fruit extract as the carbon source and ammonia solution as the nitrogen source via a hydrothermal method. The synthesised N-CDs were employed as fluorescent probes for the highly sensitive detection of Fe3+. The linear range was 0–2 μM. The Fe3+detection mechanism of N-CDs involves the formation of a complex between Fe3+ and oxygen-containing functional groups such as hydroxyl and carboxyl groups in N-CDs, leading to the quenching of CDs fluorescence [43]. The results indicate that N-CDs can efficiently and sensitively detect Fe3+ ( Fig. 1(a)). Chang et al. utilized knotweed as the sole carbon source and ingeniously designed a rapid "turn–off" sensing platform for the ultra-sensitive detection of Fe3+ in vitro and in vivo (Fig. 1(b)). CDs with extremely high stability, ultra-low toxicity, and good biocompatibility were prepared via a one-step hydrothermal method. The obtained CDs exhibit excitation-dependent behaviour and boast a high quantum yield of up to 42 %. Due to the static quenching effect, the fluorescence of CDs is quenched, with a linear range of 0 µM–400 µM and LOD of 0.025 μM [44]. Thanks to these characteristics, CDs have been further employed for multi-colourimaging and tracking of Fe3+ fluctuations within live cells. Bioimaging of zebrafish larvae exposed to CDs confirmed the ability of CDs to efficiently circulate to other tissues and organs due to their small size. The CDs prepared by the authors were also utilized for the real-time detection of Fe3+ within the bodies of nude mice.

CDs-based fluorescent biosensors and ligands have made significant contributions to metal ion detection. Yue et al. utilized citric acid and ethylenediamine to prepare a novel type of fluorescent CDs via a hydrothermal method (Fig. 1(c)). By Modifying EGTA (a calcium ion chelator) through a secondary hydrothermal process, resulted in EGTA-modified carbon quantum dots. These carbon quantum dots exhibited bright blue fluorescence, the intensity of which sharply decreased as the calcium concentration increased. The complexation of EGTA with Ca2+ led to the aggregation of carbon quantum dots, thereby causing fluorescence quenching. Fluorescence quenching was correlated with the concentration of calcium ions, showing a good linear relationship within the range of 15–300 μM, with an LOD of 0.38 μM [45]. The results indicate that the CDs have high sensitivity and selectivity towards calcium ions. Table 1

In the specific recognition of metal ions by aptamers, the oligonucleotide single strands of the aptamer will form G-quartet complex structures with the metal ions through folding and twisting. For example, Wang et al. used trisodium citrate as the carbon source and urea as the nitrogen source to prepare nitrogen-doped CDs (CDUN) using a microwave method. The authors utilized the interaction between the aptamer (Apt) and CDUN to construct a label-free sensing platform for K+ (Fig. 1(d)). The mechanism of this detection involves binding the Apt to CDUN through intermolecular forces, decreasing the fluorescence intensity of CDUN. Upon adding K+, the Apt forms a G-quartet-K+ complex, releasing carbon quantum dots. As the concentration of K+ increases, more CDUN is detached, gradually restoring the fluorescence intensity of CDUN. By detecting the change in fluorescence intensity before and after the comparison, quantitative detection of K+ within the concentration range of 0.008–0.27 μmol/L can be achieved [46]. Similar reports include Peng et al., who chemically assembled Apt-peptide conjugates (APCs) by coupling Pb2+ aptamers with peptides using a chemical method. The APC structure domain contains a G-quartet and a Pb2+ binding peptide sequence, allowing APC to recognise the target Pb2+ at multiple sites. A fluorescent probe was synthesised by modifying FAM on the Apt of APC. Without Pb2+, the Apt of APC adopts a randomly coiled state with strong fluorescence intensity. Upon adding Pb2+, the Apt of APC specifically forms G-quartets, decreasing fluorescence intensity [47]. The results indicate that the LOD of the FAM-APC probe is 12.45 nM, and this detection method has been successfully applied to the detection of Pb2+ in horseshoe crabs, demonstrating its significant potential in practical food testing.

L-cysteine (L-Cys) is the only amino acid containing a thiol group. Thiol groups possess strong reducibility, thus L-Cys plays an indispensable role in physiological activities. A deficiency in L-Cys can lead to various syndromes such as hair depigmentation, oedema, skin lesions, liver damage, drowsiness and muscle and fat loss [57,58]. In addition, L-Cys is widely used in industrial applications such as food processing and cosmetics, making its detection crucial in clinical and industrial settings. Yang et al. prepared sulfur and nitrogen co-doped red-emissive CDs (R-CDs) using 2,5-diaminobenzenesulphonic acid as the sole carbon source via a hydrothermal method ( Fig. 2(a)). The nitrogen and sulfur doping endowed the surface of R-CDs with a unique functional group distribution, allowing them to complex with Fe3+ ions leading to fluorescence quenching. Upon adding L-Cys, competition binding between L-Cys and Fe3+ ions resulted in the fluorescence restoration of R-CDs. Based on this principle, a dual-functional sensing platform with high sensitivity for the real-time detection of Fe3+ ions and L-Cys was constructed [59]. Under optimal conditions, the LOD for Fe³⁺ is 0.27 μM, while that for L-Cys is 0.14 μM. Compared with traditional detection methods, this method saves more time and is more efficient, capable of completion within 3 minutes. Additionally, the authors utilised this sensor platform to detect Fe3+ ions and L-Cys in human serum samples, with satisfactory results.

Lin et al. utilised orange, fluorescent dual-emission CDs to detect L-glutamate (L-Glu). The authors synthesised CDs using o-phenylenediamine (OPD) and oxalic acid via a hydrothermal method (Fig. 2(b)). When excited at a wavelength of 390 nm, two emission peaks at 453 and 560 nm were observed. Upon adding L-Glu, the fluorescence intensity at 560 nm exhibited significant quenching, whereas no apparent change in the emission peak intensity at 453 nm was observed. The LOD was approximately 0.085 μM [60]. A successful analysis of L-Glu in foetal bovine serum samples was conducted with a recovery rate of 97.07 %–103.7 %. Jiao et al. prepared unlabelled ratio fluorescence N-CDs for the intrinsic ratio detection of Ag+ and glutathione (GSH) using neutral red and triethylamine as precursors via a hydrothermal method, accompanied by orange and green visual fluorescence changes (Fig. 2(c)). Upon adding Ag+, the fluorescence intensity of CDs at 618 nm decreased, and a new emission peak was observed at 532 nm, corresponding to the charge transfer from the N-Ag ligand to the metal. The fluorescence colour of the N-CDs solution changed from orange to green. When GSH was present, the charge transfer process from the N-Ag ligand to the metal was interrupted. Due to the stronger binding affinity of GSH to Ag+ than N-CDs, the fluorescence intensity at 618 nm increased whereas that at 532 nm decreased, simultaneously reverting the solution’s fluorescence from green back to orange. The LOD for GSH was 0.12 μM, demonstrating high sensitivity [61]. N-CDs exhibit excellent biocompatibility and can be a visual biosensing platform for detecting Ag+ and GSH within cells. This demonstrates the significant potential of the proposed N-CDs in biological systems.

Protein tyrosine kinase 7 (PTK7) is a membrane receptor in various cancers. Therefore, detecting PTK7 in peripheral circulation would be an effective method for early cancer diagnosis [62,63]. Ma et al. developed a signal amplification ratio fluorescence probe based on CDs and nucleic acid Apts to detect PTK7 ( Fig. 3(a)). The authors assembled y-CDs modified with Apts and cDNA-modified Fe3O4 MNPs and constructed a ratio fluorescence probe using b-CDs as an internal reference. In the absence of PTK7, the fluorescence of y-CDs is quenched by Fe3O4 and its Apt complex. Upon adding PTK7, the Apt on y-CDs binds to it, leading to the detachment from the Fe3O4 surface and gradual fluorescence recovery. The free Apts are cleaved by DNase I, further enhancing the sensitivity of the fluorescence probe by amplifying the detection signal, achieving a PTK7 LOD as low as 0.016 ng/mL [64]. This sensitive and selective fluorescence probe has been successfully used to detect PTK7 in MCF-7 cells and human serum, demonstrating tremendous potential for clinical applications.

Acetylcholinesterase (AChE) is a key enzyme in the central nervous system of animals, maintaining levels of the neurotransmitter acetylcholine (ACh) by catalysing the hydrolysis of ACh to choline. Hu et al. prepared a CD-based ratiometric fluorescent probe for the sensitive detection of AChE and butyrylcholinesterase (BChE) [65]. The reaction between OPD and Cu2+ produces 2,3-diaminophenazine (oxOPD), which has an absorption wavelength that overlaps with the emission of the CDs, and the fluorescence peak of the CDs at 460 nm is quenched through an IFE mechanism (Fig. 3(b)). Meanwhile, a new fluorescence peak at 570 nm appeared. The substrate ATCh or BTCh was hydrolysed to thiocholine in the presence of AChE or BChE, respectively, and the mercapto group can capture and strongly bind Cu2+. Then, OPD oxidation is blocked, leading to an increase in the fluorescence peak of the CDs at 460 nm and a decrease in the fluorescence peak of oxOPD at 570 nm. Also, discrimination between AChE and BChE was achieved when the respective inhibitor was added, and the LOD values of AChE and BChE activities were 0.1 and 0.04 U/L, respectively.

Alkaline phosphatase (ALP) is an important enzyme that is widely present in many tissues of the human body, particularly in the liver, bones and biliary system. Elevated levels of ALP are typically associated with liver diseases (such as hepatitis and cirrhosis), bone disorders (such as osteoporosis and Paget’s disease) and biliary obstruction. Therefore, the sensitive detection of ALP using fluorescent CDs is crucial. Wu et al. prepared green fluorescent N-CDs through hydrothermal treatment with ethylamine and catechol [66]. In the presence of ALP, PNPP is catalysed into PNP, and its maximum absorption wavelength overlaps with the excitation wavelength of the CDs, leading to the quenching of the CDs’ fluorescence through the IFE mechanism. The LOD is as low as 0.001 U/L (Fig. 3(c)). This sensing method has been successfully applied to ALP sensing in serum samples, research on ALP inhibitors and phosphatase cell imaging.

CA125 is a mucin-like glycoprotein, considered a primary biomarker for the clinical diagnosis of ovarian cancer [67]. Therefore, the sensitive detection of CA125 is of significant clinical value for the early diagnosis and treatment of ovarian cancer and other malignant tumours. Various types of analysis methods have been developed through the collaborative efforts of numerous research groups, including enzyme-linked immunosorbent assay [68], electrochemical immunoassay [69], lateral flow immunoassay [70], fluorescence immunoassay [71] and colourimetric immunoassay [72]. However, these methods suffer from issues such as cumbersome operation, lengthy processing time, and high costs, which limit their use in clinical applications. Therefore, researchers have developed a CDs-based detection method for CA125. Liu et al. first utilised pear juice as the carbon source and ethylenediamine as the nitrogen-doped precursor to prepare (N-CDs) with high fluorescence quantum yield using microwave-assisted pyrolysis technology. They systematically investigated the fluorescence enhancement mechanism of nitrogen doping. Based on the competitive adsorption of the CA125 Apt on the surface of N-CDs and the fluorescence recovery induced by desorption, they proposed a highly sensitive and selective label-free ‘turn-on’ fluorescence analysis method. Its LOD is 0.035 U/mL, and the linear range is from 0.1 to 16 U/mL [73]. Additionally, the proposed label-free ‘turn-on’ fluorescence method has been successfully applied to detecting CA125 in serum samples from both healthy individuals and patients with ovarian cancer, indicating its excellent diagnostic utility. Ghadareh et al. developed an ultra-sensitive antibody-ssDNA Apt sandwich-type fluorescence immunosensor for CA125 detection [74]. The mechanism of CA125 sensing by the proposed nanoprobe is based on the sandwich formation of fluorescent indicator CDs and AuNPs labeled PAMAM-Ab quenchers. Upon capping AuNPs, the intrinsic fluorescence of CDs is quenched, and adding CA125 lead to the closing of fluorescent indicator CDs to the AuNP surface and more decrements in the fluorescence signal of CDs ( Fig. 4(a)). Under optimal conditions, the LOD of this immunosensor is as low as 0.5 fg/mL.

Carcinoembryonic antigen (CEA) is an acidic glycoprotein with human embryonic antigen characteristics. It is an important tumour biomarker for colorectal cancer and some other cancers [75]. Hong et al. proposed a label-free sensitive detection method for CEA [76,77]. Tomato juice was used as the carbon source, and carboxyl-rich CDs synthesised using a hydrothermal method were employed for CEA detection. Initially, the abundant carboxyl groups on the surface of CDs facilitated the strong adsorption of ssDNA through π–π stacking interactions, forming CDs–Apt complexes and resulting in effective fluorescence quenching. Upon adding CEA, the strong affinity between CEA and its Apt led to the dissociation of the CEA–Apt complex from the surface of CDs, thereby restoring the fluorescence signal of CDs (Fig. 4(b)). This achieved quantitative detection of CEA concentrations in the 1–0.5 ng/mL range, with a 0.3 ng/mL LOD. Shao et al. developed a nanosensor for early lung cancer diagnosis by creating an Apt sensor based on NIR-CDs to accurately identify CEA in pleural effusion (Fig. 4(c)). DNA-modified NIR-CDs and Apt-decorated SiO2-coated gold nanorods (AuNRs) were used as building blocks for the nanosensor [78]. Due to the fluorescence resonance energy transfer (FRET) effect between AuNRs and NIR-CDs, the probe remains quenched. Only when CEA is added does the interaction between the modified Apt and CEA enable the fluorescence emission of the NIR-CDs. This nanosensor demonstrates good CEA recognition capability in real pleural effusion samples. The Apt sensor created in this study is more accurate and sensitive for complex target CEA samples, as it successfully avoids the inherent autofluorescence of biological matrices.

Prostate-specific antigen (PSA) is a single chain glycoprotein that belongs to the family of serine proteases, functionally resembling kallikrein. It serves as a vital biomarker for prostate cancer diagnosis. He et al. developed a new method for detecting PSA using an enzyme-free amplified fluorescence strategy [79]. The method is based on the catalytic hairpin assembly (CHA) coupling between CDs and graphene oxide (GO). In the absence of a target, the hairpin configuration of CDs-labelled DNA is adsorbed onto the GO surface through π–π stacking and hydrophobic interactions, leading to the quenching of CDs fluorescence via FRET between CDs and GO. Upon adding the target, the CHA circuit can be triggered to form double-stranded DNA, thereby restoring the fluorescence signal of CDs (Fig. 4(d)). This detection method exhibits good sensitivity and specificity, with an LOD for PSA of 0.22 ng/mL. The method has also been successfully applied to the sensitive detection of CEA and adenosine triphosphate, demonstrating its enormous potential in clinical diagnostics and biomedical research.

Glucose is the primary energy source for cellular metabolism and plays a crucial role in the natural growth of cells. Li et al. devised a ‘turn-on’ approach and designed a fluorescent probe for detecting glucose using Fe3O4@APBA and polyhydroxylated CDs [80]. These polyhydroxylated CDs can assemble into Fe3O4@APBA through dynamic reversible covalent bonds, forming a Fe3O4@APBA/CDs composite material. The adsorbed Fe3O4 quenches the fluorescence of cadmium sulfide. Upon adding glucose, due to the stronger affinity of Fe3O4 for cis-diols than for CDs, the polyhydroxylated CDs are released from Fe3O4, resulting in the fluorescence recovery of CDs ( Fig. 5(a)). The fluorescence intensity of CDs shows a good linear relationship with the glucose concentration. Wang et al. developed a bifunctional Fe, Co-CQD that exhibits considerable optical properties and peroxidase-mimicking catalytic activity for the specific substrate OPD under alkaline conditions. They constructed a dual-mode fluorescence and colourimetric sensing platform for glucose analysis at physiological pH [81]. This dual-mode sensing platform demonstrates good sensing performance, showing acceptable accuracy and stability even in complex serum samples (Fig. 5(b)). Moreover, the authors also established a smartphone-assisted colourimetric sensing system based on a portable visual detection kit and a smartphone-based 3D printing device, enabling on-site glucose detection in complex serum samples without requiring laboratory equipment, which has broad application prospects.

Dopamine (DA) is one of the most important neurotransmitters in the central nervous system, playing crucial roles in the cardiovascular, central nervous and endocrine systems. Zhu et al. have developed a novel fluorescence biosensor based on DA Apt-labelled CDs (Apt–CDs) and nanographene (NG) for DA determination (Fig. 5(c)). In this system, Apt–CDs are the energy donor (fluorophore) and chemical recogniser, while NG is the energy acceptor. Without DA, Apt–CDs are adsorbed on the surface of NG, leading to fluorescence quenching due to surface energy transfer. When DA is present, it binds to the Apt on the surface of CDs, forming specific spherical chain structures that detach from the NG surface, resulting in the recovery of Apt–CDs fluorescence intensity. Under optimal conditions, the fluorescence intensity of Apt–CDs increases linearly with the concentration of DA in the range of 0.10–5.00 nM, with a detection limit of 0.055 nM [82]. This method has been successfully applied to determining DA in human urine samples, yielding satisfactory results.

The components in human exhaled breath have been confirmed to be related to certain diseases. Numerous studies indicate that early warning and preliminary diagnosis of lung cancer can be achieved by detecting the concentration of isopropanol (IPA) in exhaled breath. Yang et al. designed a portable smartphone platform based on a chemically synthesised ratio fluorescence probe for the real-time and sensitive visual detection of IPA in exhaled breath [83]. Utilising classical biological reactions, the authors successfully modified NAD+ onto R-CDs to prepare single and dual-emission ratio fluorescence probes. As IPA was gradually added, the ratio probe solution exhibited a colour change from red to light blue, enabling the visual and highly sensitive detection of IPA in exhaled breath, with an LOD of 4.45 nM. Additionally, a colour recognition app can identify the corresponding colour information for quantitative detection, with an LOD as low as 8.34 nM (Fig. 5(d)). The results indicate that the sensing platform based on the ratio fluorescence probe shows great potential for the preliminary detection of lung cancer, promising further advancements in real-time/on-site detection.

Bacterial infection is one of the global hazards, with many people getting every year and millions dying as a result, significantly exacerbating the global burden of mortality. The majority of bacterial diseases are caused by toxins or infections from bacteria such as Escherichia coli, Salmonella typhi, Staphylococcus aureus, Streptococcus, Mycobacterium tuberculosis, Bacillus anthracis, and Clostridium difficile [84]. Nowadays, one of the major reasons why multidrug-resistant bacteria have become one of the greatest threats to public health is the overuse of antibiotics. A crucial and essential step in controlling pathogens, especially resistant ones, is the effective and rapid monitoring of bacterial quantities [85]. The lack of real-time detection methods is a major obstacle, limiting progress in this critical field. Traditional bacterial detection methods, while having good specificity and sensitivity, require extensive sample preparation, which is time consuming. Therefore, there is an urgent need to improve existing methods for rapid bacterial detection. Due to their low biotoxicity, high fluorescence emission intensity, small size and tunable surface chemistry, CDs have been widely applied in bacterial detection. Given their unique optical properties, fluorescent nanomaterials are increasingly important in improving bacterial pathogen sensing and detection methods.

CDs–Apt can serve as fluorescent probes for bacterial detection. Wang et al. utilised citric acid as the carbon source and prepared carboxyl-modified CDs with improved biocompatibility via a hydrothermal method. Following activation with EDC and NHS (common coupling agents), the carboxyl-modified CDs were conjugated with Apt amino groups to form CDs–Apt complexes ( Fig. 6(a)). These complexes can specifically bind to bacterial outer membrane proteins, achieving the purpose of bacterial detection. These complexes can specifically bind to bacterial outer membrane proteins, achieving the purpose of bacterial detection [86]. The authors conducted fluorescence detection using Salmonella typhimurium as a representative sample, with an LOD of 50 cfu/mL. Furthermore, they applied this method to detect the concentration of S. typhimurium in eggshells and tap water, yielding results consistent with conventional plate counting methods. The research findings indicate the promising practical application of the detection method using CDs–Apt as probes.

Zhong et al. synthesised CDs with a blue fluorescence and modified their surface with vancomycin (Fig. 6(b)). Vancomycin was conjugated to the surface of CDs through a condensation reaction, with EDC and NHS as cross-linkers. Upon the addition of S. aureus, CDs aggregated on the surface of the bacteria due to the high affinity of vancomycin for various D-Ala-D-Ala receptors, resulting in fluorescence quenching [87]. The LOD was 9.40 × 104 cfu/mL. Subsequently, the authors applied this method to detect bacteria such as Bacillus subtilis, Listeria monocytogenes, Salmonella, Pseudomonas aeruginosa, and E. coli, achieving satisfactory detection results. Similar reports include the work of Tabaraki et al., who utilised vancomycin-modified nitrogen and chlorine-doped carbon quantum dots (N, Cl-CDs) as probes for S. aureus [88]. The authors prepared NN, Cl-CDs via a microwave method, and modified them with vancomycin to form N, Cl-CDs@van on the surface of the doped CDs. Upon the addition of S. aureus, N, Cl-CDs@van adsorbed onto the surface of the bacteria, resulting in fluorescence quenching. The detection range was 102–107 cfu/mL, with an LOD as low as 101 cfu/mL (Fig. 6(c)). This fluorescence probe exhibits advantages such as rapid detection, wide linear range, low LOD, and ease of use.

The combination of nanoparticle amplification technology with immune recognition and magnetic separation has led to a novel fluorescence immunoassay method for detecting pathogens. For example, Yang et al. prepared novel CDs@BONs by the co-hydrolysation of tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS) and bis[3-(triethoxysilyl)propyl] disulfide in the presence of CDs. By using CDs@BONs as fluorescent labels, a novel signal-amplified strategy was developed for the fluorescent detection of S. aureus. Hundreds of CDs are encapsulated within each nanocapsule (Fig. 6(d)). The prepared immunofluorescent nanocapsules, namely CDs@BONs conjugated with antibodies against S. aureus, can specifically recognise S. aureus. Before fluorescence detection, the addition of NaBH4 facilitates the release of CDs from BONs [89]. Compared with traditional immunoassays using CDs as fluorescent labels, the fluorescence signal is amplified by two orders of magnitude due to the encapsulation of hundreds of CDs within each nanocapsule. CDs@BONs also hold promise for extension to other systems and detection of ultra-low concentrations of target analytes. This work provides a potential approach for the highly sensitive fluorescence detection of pathogens.

Using DNA hybridisation and FRET as the basis, Gao et al. designed a novel fluorescent aptasensor to detect P. aeruginosa (Fig. 6(e)). In the absence of the target bacteria, the complementary DNA labelled with 5-carboxyfluorescein (FAM-cDNA) hybridises with the nucleic acid Apt sequence, and the fluorescence of FAM is quenched by oxidised graphene quantum dots (GOQDs). Upon adding the target bacteria, the nucleic acid Apt specifically binds to P. aeruginosa. FAM-cDNA prefers to bind to the Apt, causing the dissociation of FAM-cDNA from GOQDs, thereby restoring the fluorescence of FAM. The aptasensor exhibits a good linear relationship with P. aeruginosa in the concentration range of 1.28 × 103–2.00 × 107 cfu/mL, demonstrating excellent selectivity. The LOD of this method is 100 cfu/mL [90]. This method exhibits an excellent response time to P. aeruginosa and can be completed within a relatively short period. It has also been successfully applied to detect P. aeruginosa in samples such as drinking water, orange juice and popsicles.

Chu et al. synthesised a novel type of doped CDs (N-CDs-GO) via a hydrothermal method. The authors utilised L-tryptophan and chlorhexidine acetate as carbon sources and selected GO as a crosslinking agent (GO extracted from Gardenia fruit, an excellent natural biopolymer crosslinking agent. GO is also utilised for its anti-inflammatory and cholagogue properties [91,92]). A multi-functional CDs was synthesised (Fig. 6(f)). Compared with N-CDs, N-CDs-GO exhibits superior biocompatibility, lower cytotoxicity and better antibacterial performance. N-CDs-GO can selectively interact with gram-positive bacteria, effectively distinguishing between gram-positive and gram-negative bacteria [93]. The research findings indicate that N-CDs-GO exhibits excellent biocompatibility, strong stability and broad-spectrum antibacterial activity. N-CDs-GO holds potential for use in bioimaging, antibacterial agents and bacterial identification to accelerate wound healing. Similarly, Yu et al. modified N-CDs with the antifungal drug amphotericin B, achieving fluorescent detection of Candida albicans [94]. After binding with CDs, amphotericin B can attach to receptors on the cell surface through ligand–receptor interactions. Leveraging the fluorescence properties of CDs, it achieves selective recognition of C. albicans.

Throughout history, humanity has continuously battled viruses, from the bubonic plague, smallpox and swine flu to Ebola and human immunodeficiency virus, and more recently, severe acute respiratory syndromes, Middle East respiratory syndrome coronavirus and Zika virus. These viruses have claimed the lives of billions and caused significant socioeconomic impacts [95]. Due to the lack of effective drugs for the clinical treatment of many pathogenic viruses, early, rapid, and accurate virus detection is crucial in preventing the spread of pathogens. Various nanomaterials have emerged as novel antiviral agents due to their unique physical and chemical properties [96–100]. Indeed, they are also widely utilised in the diagnosis of viral infections.

Pareek et al. developed an ultra-sensitive and label-free DNA-based impedimetric genosensor to detect cervical cancer HPV-18 ( Fig. 7(a)). Electrochemical analysis was performed for sensing platform characterisation and analyte detection. A single-stranded 25-mer oligonucleotide DNA probe was immobilised onto a nitrogen-doped CND-modified ITO electrode. The hybridisation event was measured by testing the complementary single-stranded DNA sequence in the samples. The sensor has an LOD of 0.405 fM. Due to the sensor’s good sensitivity to both complementary and non-complementary sequences of HPV-18, the developed genosensor shows high selectivity for HPV-18 in clinical samples [101]. The sensing platform is a rapid and selective screening method for HPV-18. Xu et al. developed a real-time immunoassay biosensor using CDs/SiO₂ nanospheres (CSNs) for the sensitive detection of severe fever with thrombocytopenia syndrome virus (SFTSV). The CSNs were prepared through a co-hydrolysis method involving TEOS and siliconised CDs. Before immobilising antibodies to capture the SFTSV, EDC was used as a coupling agent and NHS as an activating agent to activate the carboxyl terminal groups of the CSNs (Fig. 7(b)). Due to the excellent fluorescent properties of CSNs, this biosensor offers a longer analytical lifespan and good selectivity. Based on this principle, the SFTSV content in the buffer was visually observed successfully, with an LOD of 10 pg/mL [102]. This method demonstrates rapid, sensitive, and highly selective responses to SFTSV, making it suitable for the detection of actual samples.

Metals and metal oxide nanoparticles have been successfully used to detect viruses. Alzahrani et al. aimed to utilise the exciton-plasmon coupling of CDs and the rough plasmonic surface of AuNPs to develop a highly sensitive biosensor to detect norovirus (NLPs) in human serum (Fig. 7(c)). CDs bound with virus-specific antibodies interact with AuNPs in the presence of target NLPs. The plasmonic properties of AuNPs enhance the fluorescence of CDs. When NLPs are present in human serum, the fluorescence intensity of CDs is enhanced. The enhanced fluorescence response is linearly correlated up to 1 μg/mL, with a calculated LOD of 80.3 pg/mL. The research results indicate that the sensitivity of this sensor is 10 times higher than that of commercial diagnostic kits [103].

The surface plasmon resonance (SPR) effect can significantly enhance the fluorescence signal of CQDs [104]. The SPR effect is manifested by the resonance oscillation of conduction electrons on the material’s surface induced by incident photons [105]. Based on this principle, Achadu et al. developed a fluorescence platform composed of CQDs for virus detection [106]. The authors first synthesised magnetic plasma–derived molybdenum trioxide quantum dots (MP-MoO3 QDs) and CQDs. MP-MoO3 QDs exhibit tunable localised SPR effects, and the absorption spectrum of synthesised MP-MoO3 QDs overlaps with the fluorescence spectrum of CQDs. When these two quantum dots are combined, the fluorescence of CQDs is significantly enhanced due to energy transfer processes and SPR effects. During the detection process, MP-MoO3 QDs and CQDs are first separately immobilised with virus-targeting antibodies. After incubation with influenza A virus, the CQDs bind to the virus and form a core-satellite network, leading to an observable increase in fluorescence signal. By monitoring the change in fluorescence intensity, the virus content can be quantified (Fig. 7(d)). The LOD is as low as 45 pfu/mL.

Since their discovery, CDs have attracted increasing attention due to their excellent intrinsic properties such as electronic, optical, chemical, and thermal performance. As an important non-invasive optical diagnostic tool, fluorescent biosensors are widely used in disease biomarker detection, bioimaging, and point-of-care testing (POCT) due to their ultra-sensitivity and high-throughput characteristics. In this review, we summarize the latest advances in the application of CDs fluorescence probes for the detection of disease biomarkers, including sensing mechanisms, and their applications in the selective detection of metal ions, amino acids, enzymes, proteins, other biomolecules, as well as bacteria and viruses.

Although CDs have been widely applied in sensor detection, many problems and challenges remain to be addressed. (1) CDs-based fluorescent probes have broad application prospects in detecting disease biomarkers, but the issue of poor selectivity poses significant challenges that severely impact their clinical effectiveness. Various design strategies and sensing mechanisms can be explored, including surface functionalisation, synthesising specific targeting probes and optimising sensing mechanisms to enhance selectivity. Future research should focus on addressing the problem of poor selectivity to promote the practical application of CDs-based fluorescent probes in disease detection. (2) Although CDs-based fluorescent biosensors have made some progress in the early diagnosis of diseases, they are highly dependent on expensive large-scale instruments and specialised operating procedures. Therefore, there is a need to develop intelligent CDs sensors that integrate CDs probes with portable and rapid detection devices for the real-time detection of disease-related biomarkers in biological samples (such as blood and urine). This would greatly facilitate self-monitoring for patients and home care. By utilising cloud computing and big data analysis technologies, real-time monitoring data can be processed to identify potential disease trends and provide a basis for clinical decision-making. (3) To minimise light scattering and tissue damage while achieving deeper optical penetration for imaging and image-guided therapy, CDs excited by deep red to near-infrared light are required. However, current research on CDs for biological imaging mainly focuses on fluorescence imaging, which has limited value in biological tissues. Therefore, it is essential to develop multimodal imaging probes that integrate CDs with fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI), and computed tomography (CT) to enhance the accuracy and reliability of early disease diagnosis. By combining different imaging modalities, lesions can be observed at various levels. (4) The luminescence mechanism of CDs is still controversial, making it difficult to design effective fluorescence probes and understand sensing mechanisms. Further experimental and theoretical research is needed to explore new luminescence mechanisms based on existing fluorescence mechanisms. (5) With the commercialisation and industrial development of CDs sensor technology, it will be necessary to establish relevant standardisation and normalisation systems, including regulations for the quality control of CDs materials, sensor performance evaluation, safety assessment, and other aspects.

Bing Li: Validation, Supervision. Haoqi Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Visualization, Validation, Supervision, Software, Resources, Project administration, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Qingmei Zhang: Writing – review & editing, Project administration, Funding acquisition, Formal analysis, Data curation, Conceptualization. Naihui Li: Investigation, Data curation. Guoqing Yang: Investigation, Data curation. Zewei Cheng: Project administration, Data curation. Xiujuan Du: Supervision, Project administration. Lingxiang Sun: Supervision. Wei Wang: Validation, Supervision.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We acknowledge the funding support from the National Natural Science Foundation of China (NO. 82373107).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.colsurfb.2024.1143607.
